text: Robotic splenectomy. This is the case of a seventy-year-old female who presented to emergency room after a fall. The patient had multiple medical problems and included spinal stenosis for which she had an MRI five years prior and demonstrated an splenic cyst measuring eight centimeters as an incidental finding. A CT scan performed in the emergency room demonstrated that the splenic lesion had grown to a size of thirteen centimeters.

Representative CT scan images are shown. The CT scan results were consistent with a hemangioma of the spleen with internal bleeding. Having that into consideration and the history of frequent falls, we're concerned for massive bleeding if the spleen would rupture after a fall. And therefore, an elective splenectomy was offered to the patient.
She received post-splenectomy vaccines. She was cleared by medicine, pulmonology, and cardiology. And the patient was then brought to the operating room for an elective surgery.

The surgeon begins dissection by accessing the lesser sac, taking down the gastrocolic ligament along the greater curvature of the stomach until reaching the upper pole of the spleen. The large splenic mass is exposed, and attachments to the splenic lesion are bluntly dissected and sealed using a vessel sealer device. The splenocolic ligament is taken down from medial to lateral, followed by mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The robotic third arm provides medial retraction of the colon, enhancing access for lung dissection in difficult corners. The surgeon continues dissection medially along the gastrocolic ligaments using a vessel sealer, with the large splenic lesion visibly pushing surrounding tissues away, facilitating the dissection process.

The surgeon uses robotic shears to dissect and isolate the splenic vein and artery, following the impression of the splenic artery. Multiple branches of the vessels are encountered during the dissection.

Endocyanin green is injected intravenously, and the firefly function of the robotic system is activated to visualize the vascular distribution at the splenic hilum. The surgeon dissects, isolates, clips, and transects the vessels supplying the spleen using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the spleen by taking down the splenophranic ligament. The spleen is then placed into an extra large endocatch bag and extracted through a previous C-section incision.

This picture shows the size of the specimen removed. The patient did well postoperatively. She was discharged on post of day five and following the office. Initial pathology report demonstrated an organizing clot, but an increased number of B cells were found. Therefore, the second opinion was requested. The final pathology report confirmed a low-grade lymphoproliferative disorder.
